serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of deep-fried fish fillet with sweet and sour sauce is approximately 300 g, comprising a battered and deep-fried white fish fillet topped with a sweet and sour sauce containing vegetables and pineapple, served as a main dish.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, battered or breaded, fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15222) and Thai sweet and sour sauce component estimation from USDA Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Thai deep-fried fish fillet with sweet and sour sauce entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for battered/breaded fried fish (NDB 15222) combined with proportional contribution of sweet and sour sauce components (sugar, vinegar, tomato, pineapple, fish sauce) estimated from USDA Foundation Foods. The composite represents approximately 65% fried fish fillet and 35% sweet and sour sauce by weight. Energy and macros are weighted accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on battered fried fish and sweet and sour sauce carbohydrate profile
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai deep-fried fish fillet with sweet and sour sauce. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the composite carbohydrate profile: wheat flour batter (GI ~65, white wheat flour reference) and sweet and sour sauce dominated by sugar and pineapple (GI ~50–60). Weighted composite GI of approximately 55 is defensible. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (55 × 41.4 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 22.8. Source: Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021: a systematic review. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of this dish as a single 300 g serving provides approximately 1,260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced fish sauce or sauce on the side is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index but moderate glycemic load from wheat flour batter and sugar-rich sweet and sour sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that while this dish has a low glycemic index (55), a standard 300 g serving carries a glycemic load of approximately 22.8 (moderate-high), driven by the wheat flour batter and added sugar in the sweet and sour sauce; pairing with non-starchy vegetables and limiting rice accompaniment can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and caloric density; sodium load is elevated
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation given its deep-fried preparation method contributing approximately 9.2 g fat per 100 g and a high sodium content; choosing steamed or grilled fish preparations would be a lower-risk alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep-fried Fish Fillet with Sweet and Sour Sauce (Thai: ปลาทอดราดซอสเปรี้ยวหวาน, romanized: Pla Thot Rat Sot Priao Wan) is a popular Thai main dish consisting of a white fish fillet — commonly snapper, tilapia, or barramundi — coated in a light wheat flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy, then topped or served alongside a vibrant sweet and sour sauce. The sauce is typically made from tomato, pineapple chunks, onion, bell pepper, sugar, vinegar, and fish sauce, creating a glossy, tangy-sweet glaze with umami depth. The dish offers a contrast of textures: a crispy exterior from the frying and a tender, flaky fish interior, complemented by the soft vegetables in the sauce. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai restaurants such as Sanook Kitchen and is a staple of Thai-cuisine menus. Nutritionally, a 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 555 kcal, 40.5 g protein, 27.6 g fat, and 41.4 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (approximately 1,260 mg per serving) due to fish sauce and seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. While it has a low glycemic index (55), the moderate glycemic load of approximately 22.8 per serving warrants attention for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The fish component provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus. This dish is not suitable for individuals with fish or gluten allergies.

confidence_reason: No exact match for Thai deep-fried fish fillet with sweet and sour sauce was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy battered/breaded fried fish data combined with proportional sweet and sour sauce component estimation. GI is an analogous estimate based on the composite carbohydrate profile from the University of Sydney GI Database reference values. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. The input food name '411D Deep-fried Fish Fillet w Sweet and' is a truncated menu code from field survey data (Sanook Kitchen - Funan). The canonical name has been expanded to 'Deep-fried Fish Fillet with Sweet and Sour Sauce' based on standard Thai menu interpretation. 2. Nutrient values are adapted estimates; no exact entry exists in primary regional databases. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (battered/breaded fried fish, NDB 15222) was used as the primary anchor with sauce component adjustment. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for battered fried fish and is not estimable from sauce components. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for the composite dish. 5. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Halal status is assumed based on typical Thai restaurant preparation in Singapore; kosher status is not confirmed. 7. The dish is served at Sanook Kitchen - Funan (field survey data, price SGD 17.44). 8. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule is not triggered.
